input.topic: How commercial cleaning companies can win facilities manager contracts
input.coreInsight: Facilities managers shortlist cleaning contractors based on responsiveness and specificity — generic outreach gets ignored. A 3-email sequence that references their industry, building size, and cleaning frequency converts at 3–5x the rate of a generic pitch.
input.targetAudience: Facilities managers in offices, healthcare, and education
input.cta: Book a free cold email strategy call at cleanreach.co.uk

linkedInPosts.hook: Facilities managers don't ignore bad cleaning companies. They ignore bad emails.
linkedInPosts.body:
Most commercial cleaning outreach fails before it's even read.

Not because the company isn't good enough.
Because the email looks like every other email in their inbox.

"We offer professional commercial cleaning services at competitive rates."

That line lands in 40 inboxes a week.

Here's what facilities managers actually respond to:

An email that shows you understand their building.
Their industry.
The specific cleaning problems that come with it.

A healthcare FM worries about cross-contamination protocols.
A school bursar worries about after-hours access and term-time scheduling.
An office FM worries about complaints from tenants and missed visits.

When your first email speaks to one of those concerns — by name, in plain language — it doesn't look like outreach.

It looks like you already know their world.

That's when you get replies.

It looks like you already know their world.

That's when you get replies.

linkedInPosts.hook: 7 reasons your cold emails to facilities managers aren't converting — and what to fix first.
linkedInPosts.body:
Most cleaning companies send emails. Very few win contracts from them.

Here's what separates the ones that do:

1. They lead with a specific problem, not a company intro.
   FMs don't care who you are on email one. They care whether you understand their site.

2. They mention building type or sector in the subject line.
   "Cleaning for multi-tenant office blocks in [City]" outperforms "Commercial Cleaning Services" every time.

3. They reference cleaning frequency realistically.
   Generic emails say "regular cleaning services." Specific emails say "5-day-a-week daytime cleans for open-plan offices."

4. They follow up — twice.
   Most replies come on email two or three. Sending one email and stopping is leaving contracts on the table.

5. They have one clear ask.
   Not "let us know if you're interested." Try: "Would a 10-minute call this week make sense?"

6. They don't attach a brochure to email one.
   Attachments trigger spam filters and kill open rates. Save it for the reply.

7. They track what's working.
   Open rates, reply rates, which sectors respond — without this data, you're guessing.

linkedInPosts.hook: She sent 200 emails. Got 2 replies. Then she changed one thing.
linkedInPosts.body:
Sarah runs a commercial cleaning company in the East Midlands.

Good operation. Reliable team. Competitive on price.

She spent two evenings building a list of facilities managers — offices, schools, a couple of healthcare sites.

Sent 200 emails over three weeks.

Two replies. Both dead ends.

She came to us convinced cold email didn't work for cleaning.

We read through her sequence.

Every email opened the same way:
"Hi [Name], I'm reaching out to introduce [Company Name]..."

Then a paragraph about her services.
Then a list of sectors she covered.
Then a line about competitive rates.
Then a brochure attached.

It wasn't a bad email. It was just invisible.

We rebuilt the sequence around three things:
— The specific building type she was targeting
— A cleaning problem common to that sector
— A single, low-friction next step

Email one referenced the challenges of cleaning open-plan offices during occupied hours.

Within two weeks of sending the new sequence, she had 6 replies and 3 calls booked.

Same list. Same effort. Different result.

Specificity is the difference between ignored and shortlisted.

linkedInPosts.hook: Stop trying to stand out by shouting louder. Facilities managers don't want the most impressive pitch. They want the least risky contractor.
linkedInPosts.body:
Every cleaning company owner I speak to is trying to win FMs over with the same things:

Lower prices.
More accreditations listed.
Bigger client logos.
A glossier brochure.

None of that is wrong. But none of it is what closes contracts.

Facilities managers aren't looking for the most impressive cleaning company.

They're looking for the one least likely to cause them a problem.

A missed clean means a complaint from a tenant or headteacher.
An unreliable contractor means their phone rings on a Monday morning.
A cheap quote that collapses in month three means they're re-tendering mid-year.

Their job is to avoid problems — not to find deals.

So the cleaning companies that win FM contracts aren't always the cheapest or the shiniest.

They're the ones who communicate clearly in the pitch phase.
Who reference specific details about the site.
Who follow up reliably.
Who respond fast.

Responsiveness in your outreach signals reliability in your service.

If you want to win more FM contracts, stop competing on noise.
Start competing on trust signals.

linkedInPosts.hook: How to write a 3-email cold sequence that gets facilities managers to reply — step by step.
linkedInPosts.body:
Most cleaning companies send one email and wait.

Facilities managers are busy. Their inbox is full. One email isn't enough.

Here's how to build a 3-email sequence that converts at 3–5x the rate of a generic pitch:

---

EMAIL 1 — The Specific Problem Email
Send day 1.

Subject: Cleaning for [sector] sites in [City]

Open with a problem specific to their industry — not a company intro.

Example for office FM:
"Managing cleaning across occupied open-plan offices usually means one of two things — either you're running evening cleans that no one checks, or you're handling tenant complaints about standards."

Then: one sentence on who you are and what you do.
Close with: "Worth a quick call this week to see if we'd be a fit?"

---

EMAIL 2 — The Follow-Up With Evidence
Send day 4–5.

Don't apologise for following up.

Add one proof point — a relevant site type you service, a contract retained for 3+ years, a sector-specific accreditation.

Keep it to 4 lines maximum.

---

EMAIL 3 — The Short Close
Send day 9–10.

3 lines only:
— Acknowledge it might not be the right time
— Leave the door open
— Make it easy to reply with one word

Example: "If you're locked into a contract for now, happy to reconnect closer to renewal. Worth me keeping in touch?"

---

That's it. Three emails. Specific, short, persistent.

blogArticle.introduction:
Most commercial cleaning companies send the same pitch to every facilities manager on their list. Same subject line. Same generic paragraph about 'professional cleaning services'. Same result — silence.

Facilities managers receive dozens of supplier emails every week. They are not looking for another cleaning company. They are looking for a reason to switch — or a contractor who clearly understands their building, their compliance requirements, and their operational pressures.

This article shows you exactly how to build a 3-email cold outreach sequence that speaks directly to facilities managers in offices, healthcare, and education. You will learn why generic outreach fails, what facilities managers actually respond to, and how to structure each email to move a prospect from cold contact to booked site visit. If you want predictable pipeline growth from recurring commercial contracts, this is where you start.

sections.heading: Why Facilities Managers Ignore Most Cleaning Company Outreach
sections.content:
Facilities managers are accountable for building compliance, contractor performance, and service continuity. When a cleaning contract goes wrong, they answer for it. That accountability makes them cautious — and specific.

When your cold email arrives talking about 'reliable, professional cleaning at competitive rates', it tells them nothing useful. They cannot assess risk, fit, or competence from a generic pitch. So they delete it and move on.

What actually gets their attention is specificity. An email that references their sector — whether that is a multi-floor corporate office, an NHS-registered healthcare facility, or a local authority school — signals that you understand the environment you would be cleaning. An email that mentions cleaning frequency, compliance standards like COSHH or BS EN 13549, or auditable quality systems signals that you are a professional contractor, not a price-driven commodity.

**The gap most cleaning companies miss:** facilities managers are not buying cleaning. They are buying reliability, accountability, and reduced risk. Your cold email needs to speak to those priorities — not your equipment or your years in business.

sections.heading: The 3-Email Sequence That Wins Facilities Manager Contracts
sections.content:
This sequence is designed around one principle: earn the right to a conversation by demonstrating sector knowledge before you ask for anything.

Each email has a specific job. Together, they build familiarity, credibility, and a low-friction path to a site visit.

### Email 1: The Sector-Specific Introduction (Day 1)

**Goal:** Get opened, get read, earn a reply or a follow-up read.

Subject line examples:
- *Cleaning contracts for [sector] facilities in [city] — quick question*
- *[Company name] — do you review your cleaning contractor annually?*

Keep the body to four short paragraphs:

1. One sentence on who you are and where you operate.
2. One sentence referencing their specific sector — office buildings over a certain square footage, healthcare sites requiring CQC-aligned cleaning protocols, or school sites with term-time and deep-clean requirements.
3. One sentence on a relevant outcome you have delivered for a similar client — not a generic claim, a specific result. 'We reduced cleaning-related complaint tickets by 40% for a 12,000 sq ft office in [city]' is worth ten bullet points about your services.
4. A single, low-commitment ask: would they be open to a 10-minute call to see if there is a fit?

**Critical rule:** Never attach a brochure to email one. It increases spam filter hits and reduces reply rates. Your goal is a reply, not a download.

### Email 2: The Value-Add Follow-Up (Day 4–5)

**Goal:** Re-engage non-responders without being pushy. Add something useful.

Most facilities managers do not reply to email one because they are busy — not because they are uninterested. Email two gives you a second chance by leading with insight rather than a repeated ask.

Options for value-add content:
- A practical observation: 'Most office facilities managers we speak to in [city] are managing cleaning specs written three or four years ago — before hybrid working changed occupancy patterns significantly.'
- A relevant compliance note: 'Healthcare clients often ask us about colour-coded cleaning systems for cross-contamination control — happy to share how we document this for CQC reviews if useful.'
- A simple question tied to their building type: 'Are you currently managing cleaning in-house or through a contractor?'

Close with the same low-commitment ask from email one. Keep the entire email under 120 words.

### Email 3: The Direct Close (Day 10–12)

**Goal:** Get a yes or a no. Clear the pipeline.

This email is short — five to eight sentences maximum. Acknowledge that you have been in touch twice and that you do not want to keep following up if the timing is wrong. Then make one final, specific offer.

'If you are reviewing your cleaning contractor in the next six months, I would be glad to put together a no-obligation specification and quote for your site. If the timing is not right, no problem at all — I will leave you to it.'

This approach respects their time, positions you as professional rather than desperate, and often generates replies from prospects who were simply waiting for the right moment. A 'not right now but keep us in mind' reply is a warm lead. Log it, follow up in 90 days.

**Sequence performance benchmark:** A well-built 3-email sequence targeting facilities managers with sector-specific copy typically achieves a 35–50% open rate and a 3–8% reply rate. Generic single-email blasts typically achieve under 1% replies.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes That Kill Your Reply Rate
sections.content:
Even cleaning companies with strong service offerings lose contracts before they start because of avoidable outreach errors.

**Mistake 1: Targeting the wrong contact.**
In smaller organisations, the facilities manager is the decision-maker. In larger ones — multi-site corporate offices, hospital trusts, academy chains — procurement decisions sit with a head of estates, a procurement officer, or a property director. Research before you send. Sending a cold email to someone who cannot say yes is wasted effort.

**Mistake 2: Leading with price.**
Mentioning 'competitive rates' or 'cost savings' in a cold email positions you as a commodity before you have established any credibility. Price is a conversation for the site visit. Lead with outcomes and sector knowledge first.

**Mistake 3: Sending from a cold domain.**
If your email domain is new or has no sending history, your emails will hit spam folders regardless of how good the copy is. Warm your sending domain properly — use tools like Instantly or Lemlist to build domain reputation before launching sequences at scale.

**Mistake 4: Personalising the name but nothing else.**
Merge tags that insert a first name are not personalisation. Facilities managers see through this immediately. True personalisation means referencing their building type, sector compliance requirements, or a recent news item about their organisation. That level of specificity takes more time per prospect — but the conversion rate justifies it.

**Mistake 5: Giving up after one email.**
Data consistently shows that the majority of cold email replies come from follow-up emails, not the first contact. If you are sending one email and waiting, you are leaving most of your pipeline on the table.

sections.heading: Tools to Build and Run Your Facilities Manager Outreach
sections.content:
You do not need a large budget or a dedicated sales team to run effective cold outreach. These tools are used by commercial cleaning companies across the UK to build consistent contract pipelines.

**Prospect Research and List Building**
- **LinkedIn Sales Navigator** — Filter by job title (Facilities Manager, Head of Estates, Property Manager), company size, and location. Build targeted lists of decision-makers before you write a single email.
- **Apollo.io** — Find verified contact emails and direct dials for facilities managers across sectors. Useful for volume prospecting when targeting office parks or industrial estates.
- **Google Maps + Companies House** — For local prospecting, search commercial addresses in your target area and cross-reference with company size and sector data.

**Email Sending and Sequencing**
- **Instantly.ai** or **Lemlist** — Automate your 3-email sequence with send-time optimisation, domain warm-up, and reply detection. Both tools allow personalisation variables beyond first name — include building type, sector, and city.
- **Mailtrack or HubSpot Sales Free** — For lower-volume outreach, these tools give you open and click tracking inside Gmail.

**CRM and Follow-Up Tracking**
- **HubSpot CRM (free tier)** — Log every prospect, track reply status, and set follow-up reminders for 90-day re-engagement cycles. Essential if you are managing more than 20 active prospects at once.
- A simple Google Sheet works for early-stage outreach — but as your pipeline grows, a proper CRM saves time and prevents leads going cold.

**One important note on volume:** More emails does not mean more contracts. A focused list of 50 highly relevant facilities managers with personalised, sector-specific copy will outperform a blast to 500 generic contacts every time. Build your list with precision before you scale.

blogArticle.conclusion:
Winning facilities manager contracts through cold email is not about volume or clever subject lines. It is about demonstrating — quickly and specifically — that you understand their building, their sector, and the operational standards they are responsible for.

The 3-email sequence outlined here works because it earns trust before it asks for anything. It gives facilities managers a reason to respond that goes beyond price comparison. And it creates a repeatable, scalable system for building a pipeline of commercial contracts — not a one-off win.

If you are currently relying on referrals and renewals to keep revenue stable, cold outreach to facilities managers is the most direct route to new recurring contracts. Start with a list of 30 to 50 relevant prospects in your area, build the sequence, and track your reply rates over 30 days.

**Ready to build a cold email system that consistently wins commercial contracts? Book a free cold email strategy call with CleanReach at [cleanreach.co.uk](https://cleanreach.co.uk) and we will review your current outreach and show you exactly where the pipeline gaps are.**
blogArticle.fullMarkdown:
# How to Win Facilities Manager Contracts With Cold Email (A 3-Step Sequence That Works)

Most commercial cleaning companies send the same pitch to every facilities manager on their list. Same subject line. Same generic paragraph about 'professional cleaning services'. Same result — silence.

Facilities managers receive dozens of supplier emails every week. They are not looking for another cleaning company. They are looking for a reason to switch — or a contractor who clearly understands their building, their compliance requirements, and their operational pressures.

This article shows you exactly how to build a 3-email cold outreach sequence that speaks directly to facilities managers in offices, healthcare, and education. You will learn why generic outreach fails, what facilities managers actually respond to, and how to structure each email to move a prospect from cold contact to booked site visit. If you want predictable pipeline growth from recurring commercial contracts, this is where you start.

---

## Why Facilities Managers Ignore Most Cleaning Company Outreach

Facilities managers are accountable for building compliance, contractor performance, and service continuity. When a cleaning contract goes wrong, they answer for it. That accountability makes them cautious — and specific.

When your cold email arrives talking about 'reliable, professional cleaning at competitive rates', it tells them nothing useful. They cannot assess risk, fit, or competence from a generic pitch. So they delete it and move on.

What actually gets their attention is specificity. An email that references their sector — whether that is a multi-floor corporate office, an NHS-registered healthcare facility, or a local authority school — signals that you understand the environment you would be cleaning. An email that mentions cleaning frequency, compliance standards like COSHH or BS EN 13549, or auditable quality systems signals that you are a professional contractor, not a price-driven commodity.

**The gap most cleaning companies miss:** facilities managers are not buying cleaning. They are buying reliability, accountability, and reduced risk. Your cold email needs to speak to those priorities — not your equipment or your years in business.

---

## The 3-Email Sequence That Wins Facilities Manager Contracts

This sequence is designed around one principle: earn the right to a conversation by demonstrating sector knowledge before you ask for anything.

Each email has a specific job. Together, they build familiarity, credibility, and a low-friction path to a site visit.

### Email 1: The Sector-Specific Introduction (Day 1)

**Goal:** Get opened, get read, earn a reply or a follow-up read.

Subject line examples:
- *Cleaning contracts for [sector] facilities in [city] — quick question*
- *[Company name] — do you review your cleaning contractor annually?*

Keep the body to four short paragraphs:

1. One sentence on who you are and where you operate.
2. One sentence referencing their specific sector — office buildings over a certain square footage, healthcare sites requiring CQC-aligned cleaning protocols, or school sites with term-time and deep-clean requirements.
3. One sentence on a relevant outcome you have delivered for a similar client — not a generic claim, a specific result. 'We reduced cleaning-related complaint tickets by 40% for a 12,000 sq ft office in [city]' is worth ten bullet points about your services.
4. A single, low-commitment ask: would they be open to a 10-minute call to see if there is a fit?

**Critical rule:** Never attach a brochure to email one. It increases spam filter hits and reduces reply rates. Your goal is a reply, not a download.

### Email 2: The Value-Add Follow-Up (Day 4–5)

**Goal:** Re-engage non-responders without being pushy. Add something useful.

Most facilities managers do not reply to email one because they are busy — not because they are uninterested. Email two gives you a second chance by leading with insight rather than a repeated ask.

Options for value-add content:
- A practical observation: 'Most office facilities managers we speak to in [city] are managing cleaning specs written three or four years ago — before hybrid working changed occupancy patterns significantly.'
- A relevant compliance note: 'Healthcare clients often ask us about colour-coded cleaning systems for cross-contamination control — happy to share how we document this for CQC reviews if useful.'
- A simple question tied to their building type: 'Are you currently managing cleaning in-house or through a contractor?'

Close with the same low-commitment ask from email one. Keep the entire email under 120 words.

### Email 3: The Direct Close (Day 10–12)

**Goal:** Get a yes or a no. Clear the pipeline.

This email is short — five to eight sentences maximum. Acknowledge that you have been in touch twice and that you do not want to keep following up if the timing is wrong. Then make one final, specific offer.

*'If you are reviewing your cleaning contractor in the next six months, I would be glad to put together a no-obligation specification and quote for your site. If the timing is not right, no problem at all — I will leave you to it.'*

This approach respects their time, positions you as professional rather than desperate, and often generates replies from prospects who were simply waiting for the right moment. A 'not right now but keep us in mind' reply is a warm lead. Log it, follow up in 90 days.

**Sequence performance benchmark:** A well-built 3-email sequence targeting facilities managers with sector-specific copy typically achieves a 35–50% open rate and a 3–8% reply rate. Generic single-email blasts typically achieve under 1% replies.

---

## Common Mistakes That Kill Your Reply Rate

Even cleaning companies with strong service offerings lose contracts before they start because of avoidable outreach errors.

**Mistake 1: Targeting the wrong contact.**
In smaller organisations, the facilities manager is the decision-maker. In larger ones — multi-site corporate offices, hospital trusts, academy chains — procurement decisions sit with a head of estates, a procurement officer, or a property director. Research before you send. Sending a cold email to someone who cannot say yes is wasted effort.

**Mistake 2: Leading with price.**
Mentioning 'competitive rates' or 'cost savings' in a cold email positions you as a commodity before you have established any credibility. Price is a conversation for the site visit. Lead with outcomes and sector knowledge first.

**Mistake 3: Sending from a cold domain.**
If your email domain is new or has no sending history, your emails will hit spam folders regardless of how good the copy is. Warm your sending domain properly — use tools like Instantly or Lemlist to build domain reputation before launching sequences at scale.

**Mistake 4: Personalising the name but nothing else.**
Merge tags that insert a first name are not personalisation. Facilities managers see through this immediately. True personalisation means referencing their building type, sector compliance requirements, or a recent news item about their organisation. That level of specificity takes more time per prospect — but the conversion rate justifies it.

**Mistake 5: Giving up after one email.**
Data consistently shows that the majority of cold email replies come from follow-up emails, not the first contact. If you are sending one email and waiting, you are leaving most of your pipeline on the table.

---

## Tools to Build and Run Your Facilities Manager Outreach

You do not need a large budget or a dedicated sales team to run effective cold outreach. These tools are used by commercial cleaning companies across the UK to build consistent contract pipelines.

### Prospect Research and List Building

- **LinkedIn Sales Navigator** — Filter by job title (Facilities Manager, Head of Estates, Property Manager), company size, and location. Build targeted lists of decision-makers before you write a single email.
- **Apollo.io** — Find verified contact emails and direct dials for facilities managers across sectors. Useful for volume prospecting when targeting office parks or industrial estates.
- **Google Maps + Companies House** — For local prospecting, search commercial addresses in your target area and cross-reference with company size and sector data.

### Email Sending and Sequencing

- **Instantly.ai** or **Lemlist** — Automate your 3-email sequence with send-time optimisation, domain warm-up, and reply detection. Both tools allow personalisation variables beyond first name — include building type, sector, and city.
- **Mailtrack or HubSpot Sales Free** — For lower-volume outreach, these tools give you open and click tracking inside Gmail.

### CRM and Follow-Up Tracking

- **HubSpot CRM (free tier)** — Log every prospect, track reply status, and set follow-up reminders for 90-day re-engagement cycles. Essential if you are managing more than 20 active prospects at once.
- A simple Google Sheet works for early-stage outreach — but as your pipeline grows, a proper CRM saves time and prevents leads going cold.

**One important note on volume:** More emails does not mean more contracts. A focused list of 50 highly relevant facilities managers with personalised, sector-specific copy will outperform a blast to 500 generic contacts every time. Build your list with precision before you scale.

---

## Start Building Your Contract Pipeline Today

Winning facilities manager contracts through cold email is not about volume or clever subject lines. It is about demonstrating — quickly and specifically — that you understand their building, their sector, and the operational standards they are responsible for.

The 3-email sequence outlined here works because it earns trust before it asks for anything. It gives facilities managers a reason to respond that goes beyond price comparison. And it creates a repeatable, scalable system for building a pipeline of commercial contracts — not a one-off win.

If you are currently relying on referrals and renewals to keep revenue stable, cold outreach to facilities managers is the most direct route to new recurring contracts. Start with a list of 30 to 50 relevant prospects in your area, build the sequence, and track your reply rates over 30 days.

---

### Ready to Build a Cold Email System That Wins Commercial Contracts?

**Book a free cold email strategy call with CleanReach.** We will review your current outreach, identify where your pipeline is leaking, and show you exactly how to structure a sequence for your target sectors and locations.

👉 [Book your free strategy call at cleanreach.co.uk](https://cleanreach.co.uk)

---

emailNewsletter.subjectLine: Why FMs ignore your cold emails
emailNewsletter.previewText: It's not your price. It's that your email could've been sent to anyone.
emailNewsletter.body:
Most facilities managers delete cleaning pitches before they finish the first line.

Not because they don't need a contractor. Because the email reads like it was written for no one in particular.

"We provide high-quality commercial cleaning services across a range of sectors." That sentence is in roughly 80% of cold outreach FMs receive. It tells them nothing about whether you understand their building, their schedule, or their compliance requirements.

Here's what actually works.

Facilities managers shortlist contractors based on two things: responsiveness and specificity. They want to know you've done your homework — that you understand whether they're running a 40,000 sq ft NHS-contracted healthcare facility or a multi-tenancy office block with weekend access restrictions.

A generic pitch signals that you'll be equally generic when something goes wrong on site.

A 3-email sequence built around specificity — referencing their sector, building type, likely cleaning frequency, and the compliance standards relevant to them — consistently converts at 3 to 5 times the rate of a standard pitch.

Email one: Show you understand their environment. Reference their sector and what that means for cleaning protocols. No pitch yet.

Email two: Introduce one specific outcome you've delivered for a similar site. Keep it to two sentences. Link to a short case study if you have one.

Email three: Make the ask. One clear, low-friction next step — a 15-minute call, a site visit, or a sample schedule.

That's it. Three emails. Sent over ten to fourteen days. Focused entirely on their world, not yours.

If your current outreach is getting low reply rates from offices, schools, or healthcare facilities, the problem almost certainly isn't your pricing or your service. It's that nothing in your email makes the FM feel like it was written for them.

Book a free cold email strategy call and we'll review your current outreach, identify exactly where it's losing FMs, and map out a sequence built around your target sectors.

CleanReach — helping commercial cleaning companies win predictable contracts.

gbpPosts.body:
Facilities managers don't have time for unreliable contractors.

If you manage a multi-site office, healthcare facility, or education building in [CITY], you need a contract cleaning service that shows up on schedule, reports issues without being asked, and scales with your building's needs.

That's what separates a professional commercial cleaning company from a one-size-fits-all operation.

Our contract cleaning services are built around your building — not a generic checklist. We work with facilities managers to agree cleaning frequencies, access requirements, and quality benchmarks before we start. You get a named point of contact, written service reports, and zero chasing.

Whether you manage 2,000 sq ft or 20,000 sq ft, your cleaning specification should reflect your building's footfall, usage, and compliance needs — not a standard template.

If your current contractor isn't delivering that level of detail, it's worth getting a second quote.

We cover offices, healthcare sites, schools, and commercial properties across [CITY] and surrounding areas.
gbpPosts.cta: Request a commercial cleaning quote tailored to your site at cleanreach.co.uk

gbpPosts.body:
A facilities manager at a [CITY] office complex was receiving three different cleaning contractors' quotes — all generic, all similar in price.

What made the difference wasn't the lowest number. It was specificity.

One contractor came back with a proposal that referenced the building's floor count, the high-traffic reception area, and a suggested five-day cleaning schedule with monthly deep-clean provisions. It also included a clear escalation process for reactive cleaning requests.

That contractor won the contract — and has held it for two years.

Facilities managers shortlist office cleaning contractors who demonstrate they've actually thought about the building. Vague promises don't win recurring contracts. Detailed, site-specific proposals do.

We work with facilities and property managers across [CITY] to build cleaning specifications that reflect how their buildings actually operate. Our clients aren't just buying janitorial services — they're buying reliability, consistency, and a team that understands commercial environments.

If you're reviewing your current commercial cleaning provision or tendering a new contract, we're ready to put together a proposal specific to your site.
gbpPosts.cta: Get a site-specific proposal from our commercial cleaning team — visit cleanreach.co.uk

gbpPosts.body:
Most facilities managers in [CITY] tell us the same thing: their previous contractor was fine at first, then standards slipped and communication dried up.

It's one of the most common problems in commercial cleaning — and it's entirely preventable with the right contractor structure.

Here's what reliable facilities cleaning services actually look like in practice:

— A cleaning schedule built around your building's occupancy hours
— A dedicated supervisor accountable for your site's output
— Monthly quality audits with written sign-off
— A direct line for reactive or emergency cleaning requests
— COSHH-compliant products and trained, vetted staff

We've worked with offices, schools, and healthcare sites across [CITY] long enough to know that facilities managers don't want to manage their cleaning contractor. They want to trust them.

Our role is to remove cleaning from your weekly problem list — not add to it.

If you're based in [CITY] and currently tendering for commercial cleaning services, or if your existing contract is up for review, we're happy to carry out a free site assessment and provide a written proposal within 48 hours.
gbpPosts.cta: Book a free site assessment with our [CITY] commercial cleaning team at cleanreach.co.uk

videoScripts.hook: Facilities managers are ignoring your cold emails — here's why.
videoScripts.problem: Most cleaning companies send the same generic pitch to every facilities manager they contact. 'We offer flexible schedules, competitive rates, fully insured.' Sound familiar? That email goes straight to the bin. Facilities managers manage multiple buildings, multiple contractors, and zero patience for vague promises.
videoScripts.solution: What actually gets a response is specificity. Reference their industry. Mention their building size. Acknowledge their cleaning frequency. A 3-email sequence built around those three things — industry, building, frequency — shows you've done your homework. Email one opens the door. Email two adds value. Email three asks for the meeting. That's it.
videoScripts.proof: Cleaning companies using this sequence are seeing 3 to 5 times more replies than a generic pitch — because facilities managers shortlist on responsiveness and relevance. If your email looks like it was written for them, not a mailing list, you stand out immediately.
videoScripts.cta: If you want help building that sequence for your business, book a free cold email strategy call at cleanreach.co.uk. Link's in the comments.

videoScripts.hook: Your cold email is why you're not winning facilities manager contracts.
videoScripts.problem: If your outreach says 'competitive rates, fully insured, flexible' — you sound like every other cleaning company in their inbox. Facilities managers delete those in seconds.
videoScripts.solution: Here's what works. Three emails. Email one — reference their building type and industry. Healthcare, education, offices — they all have different needs. Show you know that. Email two — give them something useful. A cleaning frequency guide, a checklist, anything relevant. Email three — short ask. 'Would a site visit make sense this month?' That's your whole sequence.
videoScripts.proof: This approach converts 3 to 5 times better than a generic pitch because it's specific. And specific means credible.

Now you're on their radar as someone worth talking to. Email three — that's where you ask. Keep it short. 'Would it make sense to have a quick call or site visit this month?' One question. Easy to say yes to.

coldEmailAngles.openingLine: Most facilities managers we speak to in [City] are stuck managing a contractor who's inconsistent — and spending time chasing problems they shouldn't have to.
coldEmailAngles.painPoint: Facilities managers waste hours following up on missed cleans, poor standards, and unreliable communication — pulling them away from higher-priority building operations.
coldEmailAngles.valueProposition: We work exclusively with [Industry] facilities across [City], offering fixed schedules, a named account contact, and monthly compliance reports so you always have oversight without the chasing.
coldEmailAngles.cta: Would it be worth a 15-minute call to see if we're a fit for [Company]?
coldEmailAngles.fullEmail:
Hi [First Name],

Most facilities managers in [City] we speak to are managing a cleaning contractor who's inconsistent — and spending time chasing issues they shouldn't have to.

We work with [Industry] facilities across [City], providing fixed schedules, a named point of contact, and monthly compliance reports.

No chasing. No gaps. Full oversight.

Would it be worth a 15-minute call to see if we're a good fit for [Company]?

Best,
[Sender Name]
coldEmailAngles.targetSegment: Facilities managers in offices and healthcare buildings with existing cleaning contracts that are underperforming

coldEmailAngles.subjectLine: How we helped [Industry] cut cleaning issues
coldEmailAngles.openingLine: We recently helped a [Industry] facilities team in [City] reduce cleaning-related complaints by standardising their contractor schedule and introducing weekly sign-off checks.
coldEmailAngles.painPoint: Facilities managers in [Industry] settings face recurring hygiene and compliance issues when cleaning contractors aren't aligned to the specific demands of their building type and usage.
coldEmailAngles.valueProposition: We specialise in [Industry] facilities and build cleaning programmes around your occupancy patterns, compliance requirements, and reporting needs — not a generic template.
coldEmailAngles.cta: Happy to share what that looked like if it's relevant to [Company] — would a quick call work?
coldEmailAngles.fullEmail:
Hi [First Name],

We recently helped a [Industry] facilities team in [City] cut cleaning-related complaints by standardising their contractor schedule and adding weekly sign-off checks.

If [Company] runs into similar issues — inconsistent standards, gaps in coverage, or contractors who don't understand [Industry] compliance requirements — it might be worth a conversation.

We build programmes specifically for [Industry] facilities, not one-size-fits-all contracts.

Happy to share what that looked like — would a quick call work?

Best,
[Sender Name]
coldEmailAngles.targetSegment: Facilities managers in healthcare and education settings where compliance and consistency are non-negotiable

coldEmailAngles.openingLine: I'll keep this short — we provide commercial cleaning contracts for [Industry] facilities in [City] and I wanted to ask if you're open to a new contractor conversation.
coldEmailAngles.painPoint: Facilities managers are busy and rarely have time to evaluate new suppliers unless the approach is relevant and respects their time from the first contact.
coldEmailAngles.valueProposition: We cover [City] exclusively, work with [Industry] buildings, and can turn around a site-specific quote within 48 hours — no generic pricing, no pushy sales process.
coldEmailAngles.cta: Are you currently contracted, or would it be useful to compare options?
coldEmailAngles.fullEmail:
Hi [First Name],

I'll keep this short.

We provide commercial cleaning contracts for [Industry] facilities across [City] and wanted to ask if you're open to a conversation about your current setup at [Company].

We work specifically with [Industry] buildings and can turn around a site-specific quote within 48 hours — no generic pricing.

Are you currently contracted, or would it be useful to compare options?

Either way, happy to hear where things stand.

Best,
[Sender Name]
coldEmailAngles.targetSegment: Facilities managers and office managers at mid-to-large commercial premises who are either out of contract or approaching renewal
